content.text: ## Commit 3 — Extract draft-checkpoint lifecycle (resume + write) to lazy ref

content.content:
213	  On a numbered choice, replace `{name}` with the chosen alternate. On Enter, fall through to step-05's overwrite gate. On any other input, treat as a new candidate name and re-run the collision check against it.
214	
215	- Headless: log `"warn: skill name '{name}' collides with existing brief at {path}"` and proceed; the existing-brief overwrite policy in step-05 §2b is the canonical gate (HALT with `overwrite-cancelled` unless `force` was supplied).
216	
217	**Portfolio-similarity check.** When the flow is interactive AND forge tier is `Deep` AND `tools.qmd` is true in `forge-tier.yaml`, load `{portfolioSimilarityCheckFile}` and follow the procedure there to catch semantic near-duplicates that exact-name collision misses. Otherwise (headless, or tier below Deep, or qmd unavailable) skip the load — the check does not run.
218	
219	**Draft-resume check (interactive only).** After the name is confirmed, also check for an in-progress draft at `{forge_data_folder}/{name}/.brief-draft.json` (a step-01 §7 checkpoint from a previous run that did not reach step-05). If the file exists AND no `skill-brief.yaml` sits beside it (a finished brief uses the same dir), present:
220	
221	```
222	**An in-progress draft for `{name}` was found** (last updated: {mtime}).
223	  [Y] Resume from the saved draft (jump to §8 with prior answers restored)
224	  [N] Start fresh (delete the draft and re-gather)
225	```
226	
227	On `[Y]`: load the JSON, restore the captured fields (target_repo, source_type, source_authority, target_version, doc_urls, intent, scope_hint, description), and jump directly to §8 — **skip the rest of §6, §7, and §7b**. The restored `description` is authoritative and does not need re-synthesis (re-running §7b would overwrite the user's previously accepted description with a fresh candidate). The user can still revise any field at step-04. On `[N]`: delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally. In headless mode this check is skipped — drafts are an interactive-only convenience and the headless path runs to completion in a single invocation.
228	
229	### 7. Summarize Gathered Intent
230	
231	"**Here's what I've captured:**
232	
233	- **Target:** {repo URL or path}
234	- **Intent:** {user's intent summary}
235	- **Scope hints:** {any hints, or "None — we'll define scope after analysis"}
236	- **Skill name:** {confirmed name}
237	- **Source type:** {source or docs-only}
238	- **Source authority:** {official/community/internal}
239	{If target_version set:}
240	- **Target version:** {target_version} (user-specified)
241	{If doc_urls collected:}
242	- **Doc URLs:** {count} supplemental documentation URLs
243	- **Forge tier:** {tier}
244	
245	Ready to analyze the target repository?"
246	
247	**Draft checkpoint (interactive only).** After the user confirms the summary, persist the captured state to `{forge_data_folder}/{skill-name}/.brief-draft.json` so a session interruption between here and step-05 doesn't force a full re-gather. Write a single JSON object with the captured fields (target_repo, source_type, source_authority, target_version, doc_urls, intent, scope_hint, description-after-§7b, the inferred forge_tier and tier_source) atomically (write to `.brief-draft.json.tmp` then rename). The file is removed by step-05 §4 after the final brief writes successfully (a draft only exists while the workflow is in flight). In headless mode skip this checkpoint — the run completes in a single invocation, no resume is meaningful.
248	
249	### 7b. Synthesize Skill Description
250	
251	The schema's `description` field is 1-3 sentences and surfaces in skill registries — it must exist by the time step-04 presents the brief. Synthesize it explicitly here, while the user's intent is fresh, instead of letting it fall out implicitly later.
252	
253	Compose a candidate 1-3 sentence description from the gathered material. **Write like a human library maintainer would** — what does an agent get from this skill, and when should it route here? Two facts must come through (what the skill is, when to use it); everything else is voice. Resist filling in the same skeleton every time.
254	
255	Load `{descriptionVoiceExamplesFile}` for the five voice examples (range of acceptable leads, structures, and trigger phrasings) and the "do not template-stamp" guidance, then compose in that spirit. The asset documents what "in that spirit" means; the gathered material to draw on is the target repo, the user's intent, the version if set, and any scope hints.
256	
257	Present:
258	
259	"**Proposed skill description:**
260	
261	> {synthesized description}
262

input.content:
# Draft Checkpoint Lifecycle

The `.brief-draft.json` file at `{forge_data_folder}/{skill-name}/.brief-draft.json` is a step-01 in-flight-state checkpoint. It exists only while the workflow has progressed past §7 but not yet completed step-05 — once the final brief writes successfully, step-05 §4 removes it.

**Headless mode skips this entire lifecycle** — the run completes in a single invocation, so no resume is meaningful and no checkpoint is written.

The two halves of the lifecycle (resume on entry, write on §7 confirmation) form a pair. This file documents both so a single load covers them.

## Half 1 — Resume Check (loaded from §6 after name confirmation)

After the skill name is confirmed in §6, check for an in-progress draft at `{forge_data_folder}/{name}/.brief-draft.json`. **Only present the resume prompt** when the file exists AND no `skill-brief.yaml` sits beside it (a finished brief uses the same dir; if a finished brief exists, the draft is stale and step-05's overwrite gate is the right control point).

When the precondition is met, present:

```
**An in-progress draft for `{name}` was found** (last updated: {mtime}).
  [Y] Resume from the saved draft (jump to §8 with prior answers restored)
  [N] Start fresh (delete the draft and re-gather)
```

### `[Y]` — Resume

Load the JSON and restore the captured fields: `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`, `target_version`, `doc_urls`, `intent`, `scope_hint`, `description`, `forge_tier`, `tier_source`. Then jump directly to §8 — **skip the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b**.

The skip rule for §7b is load-bearing: re-running §7b would overwrite the user's previously accepted `description` with a fresh candidate synthesized from the seed material. The restored `description` is authoritative.

The user can still revise any field at step-04 §3 if a refinement is needed after the full brief is visible.

### `[N]` — Start fresh

Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable, then §7 / §7b proceed in their normal order).

## Half 2 — Checkpoint Write (loaded from §7 after summary confirmation)

After the user confirms the §7 summary, persist the captured state atomically. Write a single JSON object with all of:

- `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`
- `target_version` (if set)
- `doc_urls` (if collected)
- `intent`, `scope_hint`
- `description` (the §7b accepted text)
- `forge_tier`, `tier_source` (for diagnostics)

Atomic-write protocol: write to `.brief-draft.json.tmp` first, then `mv .brief-draft.json.tmp .brief-draft.json`. The rename is atomic on a single filesystem; a partial write never becomes visible as `.brief-draft.json`.

The file is removed by step-05 §4 after the final brief writes successfully.

# Draft Checkpoint Lifecycle

The `.brief-draft.json` file at `{forge_data_folder}/{skill-name}/.brief-draft.json` is a step-01 in-flight-state checkpoint. It exists only while the workflow has progressed past §7 but not yet completed step-05 — once the final brief writes successfully, step-05 §4 removes it.

**Headless mode skips this entire lifecycle** — the run completes in a single invocation, so no resume is meaningful and no checkpoint is written.

The two halves of the lifecycle (resume on entry, write on §7 confirmation) form a pair. This file documents both so a single load covers them.

## Half 1 — Resume Check (loaded from §6 after name confirmation)

After the skill name is confirmed in §6, check for an in-progress draft at `{forge_data_folder}/{name}/.brief-draft.json`. **Only present the resume prompt** when the file exists AND no `skill-brief.yaml` sits beside it (a finished brief uses the same dir; if a finished brief exists, the draft is stale and step-05's overwrite gate is the right control point).

When the precondition is met, present:

```
**An in-progress draft for `{name}` was found** (last updated: {mtime}).
  [Y] Resume from the saved draft (jump to §8 with prior answers restored)
  [N] Start fresh (delete the draft and re-gather)
```

### `[Y]` — Resume

Load the JSON and restore the captured fields: `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`, `target_version`, `doc_urls`, `intent`, `scope_hint`, `description`, `forge_tier`, `tier_source`. Then jump directly to §8 — **skip the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b**.

The skip rule for §7b is load-bearing: re-running §7b would overwrite the user's previously accepted `description` with a fresh candidate synthesized from the seed material. The restored `description` is authoritative.

The user can still revise any field at step-04 §3 if a refinement is needed after the full brief is visible.

### `[N]` — Start fresh

Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable, then §7 / §7b proceed in their normal order).

## Half 2 — Checkpoint Write (loaded from §7 after summary confirmation)

After the user confirms the §7 summary, persist the captured state atomically. Write a single JSON object with all of:

- `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`
- `target_version` (if set)
- `doc_urls` (if collected)
- `intent`, `scope_hint`
- `description` (the §7b accepted text)
- `forge_tier`, `tier_source` (for diagnostics)

Atomic-write protocol: write to `.brief-draft.json.tmp` first, then `mv .brief-draft.json.tmp .brief-draft.json`. The rename is atomic on a single filesystem; a partial write never becomes visible as `.brief-draft.json`.

The file is removed by step-05 §4 after the final brief writes successfully.

1. **2a20c121** — Extract headless source-authority detection to `references/headless-source-authority-detection.md`. The ref documents the qmd collection enumeration, the parallel per-collection query, the 3-highest-aggregate hit presentation, the heads-up format, and the silent-skip-on-failure semantics. Step-01 §6 stubs this to a single sentence with the same gate.

Step-01 §6 stub still surfaces the load-bearing skip rule (on `[Y]` resume, skip rest of §6 / all of §7 / all of §7b and jump to §8) so the flow-control intent is reachable without loading the ref.

(I expect the answer is no — each block was already gated by its preconditions inline — but please verify.)

- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/references/headless-source-authority-detection.md`

- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/scripts/skf-forge-tier-rw.py`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md`

---

## Critical Issues

None found.

---

## Important Issues

### 1. Precondition mismatch: headless-source-authority ref lists `{headless_mode}` as an explicit precondition; the stub relies on structural implication only

Confidence: 80

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/references/headless-source-authority-detection.md`, line 5; compared with `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md`, lines 293-295.

The ref opens with three numbered preconditions, the first being "`{headless_mode}` is true". The §8 stub's detection paragraph does not restate this — it relies on the reader inferring that being inside the `GATE [default: use args]` block (which is already gated on `{headless_mode}`) covers precondition 1.

That inference is sound when an LLM reads step-01 top-to-bottom in one pass. However, the ref is written to be loadable in isolation (the precondition block at the top is meant to be a complete self-contained gate). If an LLM ever loads the ref directly — e.g., from the sidecar or a future step — and asks "should I run this?", precondition 1 would be checked correctly. If it loads the ref having entered §8 through the non-headless path (a bug elsewhere), the ref would correctly self-veto via precondition 1.

The ref is not wrong: precondition 1 adds a defense-in-depth safety check that the stub cannot provide (the stub can only prevent the load, but once the ref is loaded its own gate is the last line of defense). This is desirable, not a bug.

The actual concern is the inverse: could the stub's omission of `{headless_mode}` cause the load to happen incorrectly in interactive mode? Reading the stub:

> "**GATE [default: use args]** — If `{headless_mode}`, ... After consuming `normalized`, if `source_authority` is absent AND `source_type=source` AND `target_repo` is a GitHub URL, load `{headlessSourceAuthorityDetectionFile}`..."

The entire GATE bullet is scoped to `If {headless_mode}`. An LLM reading this linearly would not enter the GATE bullet in interactive mode. The ref load sentence is nested inside that block.

No behavior regression here, but the ref's self-documentation of `{headless_mode}` as precondition 1 is accurate and the stub's reliance on structural implication is a mild documentation asymmetry rather than a real issue. On reflection this is below the 80 threshold — lowering to informational.

Revised confidence: 72 — do not flag.

---

### 1 (revised). `[N]` start-fresh branch in Half 1 does not re-trigger the portfolio-similarity check

Confidence: 82

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/references/draft-checkpoint.md`, lines 29-31.

Half 1's `[N]` branch reads:

> "Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable, then §7 / §7b proceed in their normal order)."

The stub in step-01 §6 (lines 219-220) says:

> "On `[Y]` resume, the procedure jumps directly to §8 with prior answers restored — **the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b are skipped**. Otherwise (headless, or no draft file, or a finished brief sits beside the draft) skip the load and continue with §6 normally."

The stub's "continue with §6 normally" language matches the ref's `[N]` description. So far consistent.

The issue is sequencing. The §6 section executes in this order (reading the stub, lines 193-220):
1. Name proposal and confirmation
2. Collision check (interactive + headless)
3. Portfolio-similarity check (interactive + Deep + qmd)
4. Draft-resume check (interactive + draft exists + no finished brief)

When `[N]` is chosen at step 4, the ref says "continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable...)". But collision check (step 2) and portfolio-similarity check (step 3) have already run before the draft-resume check (step 4) fired. The `[N]` branch cannot re-run them from within the ref — it can only proceed forward in §6. There are no further §6 actions after the draft-resume check before §7.

So "continue with §6 normally" in the `[N]` path effectively means: "continue to §7 / §7b" — the collision check and portfolio-similarity check already ran and are not re-executed after deleting the draft. The description in the ref is slightly misleading (implying those checks run after `[N]`, when they already ran before the resume prompt appeared), but it is not behaviorally wrong — the net effect of `[N]` is proceeding to §7 with the same name that was just collision-checked and similarity-checked.

The practical risk: an LLM reading the ref in isolation (having loaded it because the draft exists) might interpret "continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable)" as an instruction to re-run those checks after choosing `[N]`. That would cause a redundant collision check and a second qmd similarity query with no user value, but would not cause incorrect behavior — it would just be wasteful.

This is low-harm (redundant checks, no state corruption) but is a potential behavior divergence from the inline original. Confidence: 82.

Fix: Change the `[N]` description in `draft-checkpoint.md` lines 29-31 to reflect the actual forward-continue:

```
### `[N]` — Start fresh

Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue to §7 — collision and portfolio-similarity checks
already ran before the resume prompt appeared and do not repeat.
```

---

### 2. `{tools.qmd}` field name confirmed correct; stub's prose description is unambiguous

Confidence: N/A (no issue)

`skf-forge-tier-rw.py` line 196 places `"qmd"` as a key under `tools` in the YAML output. The stub's "tools.qmd is true in `forge-tier.yaml`" matches the on-disk structure. Step-05 §5 uses the same gate ("forge tier is Deep AND QMD tool is available"). Consistent across all sites.

---

## All Other Areas — No Issues Found

**Behavior preservation (area 1):** All three reference files are strictly supersets of the inline text they replaced. The source-authority detection ref preserves all four fallback paths, adds the warn log for `gh api user` failure, and adds the case-insensitivity rationale. The portfolio-similarity ref preserves the enumeration approach, parallel query pattern, 3-highest-aggregate cut, heads-up format, and silent-skip-on-failure semantics. ## Important Issues

### 1. Precondition mismatch: headless-source-authority ref lists `{headless_mode}` as an explicit precondition; the stub relies on structural implication only

Confidence: 80

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/references/headless-source-authority-detection.md`, line 5; compared with `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md`, lines 293-295.

The ref opens with three numbered preconditions, the first being "`{headless_mode}` is true". The §8 stub's detection paragraph does not restate this — it relies on the reader inferring that being inside the `GATE [default: use args]` block (which is already gated on `{headless_mode}`) covers precondition 1.

That inference is sound when an LLM reads step-01 top-to-bottom in one pass. However, the ref is written to be loadable in isolation (the precondition block at the top is meant to be a complete self-contained gate). If an LLM ever loads the ref directly — e.g., from the sidecar or a future step — and asks "should I run this?", precondition 1 would be checked correctly. If it loads the ref having entered §8 through the non-headless path (a bug elsewhere), the ref would correctly self-veto via precondition 1.

The ref is not wrong: precondition 1 adds a defense-in-depth safety check that the stub cannot provide (the stub can only prevent the load, but once the ref is loaded its own gate is the last line of defense). This is desirable, not a bug.

The actual concern is the inverse: could the stub's omission of `{headless_mode}` cause the load to happen incorrectly in interactive mode? Reading the stub:

> "**GATE [default: use args]** — If `{headless_mode}`, ... After consuming `normalized`, if `source_authority` is absent AND `source_type=source` AND `target_repo` is a GitHub URL, load `{headlessSourceAuthorityDetectionFile}`..."

The entire GATE bullet is scoped to `If {headless_mode}`. An LLM reading this linearly would not enter the GATE bullet in interactive mode. The ref load sentence is nested inside that block.

No behavior regression here, but the ref's self-documentation of `{headless_mode}` as precondition 1 is accurate and the stub's reliance on structural implication is a mild documentation asymmetry rather than a real issue. On reflection this is below the 80 threshold — lowering to informational.

Revised confidence: 72 — do not flag.

---

### 1 (revised). `[N]` start-fresh branch in Half 1 does not re-trigger the portfolio-similarity check

Confidence: 82

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill/references/draft-checkpoint.md`, lines 29-31.

Half 1's `[N]` branch reads:

> "Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable, then §7 / §7b proceed in their normal order)."

The stub in step-01 §6 (lines 219-220) says:

> "On `[Y]` resume, the procedure jumps directly to §8 with prior answers restored — **the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b are skipped**. Otherwise (headless, or no draft file, or a finished brief sits beside the draft) skip the load and continue with §6 normally."

The stub's "continue with §6 normally" language matches the ref's `[N]` description. So far consistent.

The issue is sequencing. The §6 section executes in this order (reading the stub, lines 193-220):
1. Name proposal and confirmation
2. Collision check (interactive + headless)
3. Portfolio-similarity check (interactive + Deep + qmd)
4. Draft-resume check (interactive + draft exists + no finished brief)

When `[N]` is chosen at step 4, the ref says "continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable...)". But collision check (step 2) and portfolio-similarity check (step 3) have already run before the draft-resume check (step 4) fired. The `[N]` branch cannot re-run them from within the ref — it can only proceed forward in §6. There are no further §6 actions after the draft-resume check before §7.

So "continue with §6 normally" in the `[N]` path effectively means: "continue to §7 / §7b" — the collision check and portfolio-similarity check already ran and are not re-executed after deleting the draft. The description in the ref is slightly misleading (implying those checks run after `[N]`, when they already ran before the resume prompt appeared), but it is not behaviorally wrong — the net effect of `[N]` is proceeding to §7 with the same name that was just collision-checked and similarity-checked.

The practical risk: an LLM reading the ref in isolation (having loaded it because the draft exists) might interpret "continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable)" as an instruction to re-run those checks after choosing `[N]`. That would cause a redundant collision check and a second qmd similarity query with no user value, but would not cause incorrect behavior — it would just be wasteful.

This is low-harm (redundant checks, no state corruption) but is a potential behavior divergence from the inline original. Confidence: 82.

Fix: Change the `[N]` description in `draft-checkpoint.md` lines 29-31 to reflect the actual forward-continue:

```
### `[N]` — Start fresh

Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue to §7 — collision and portfolio-similarity checks
already ran before the resume prompt appeared and do not repeat.
```

---

### 2. `{tools.qmd}` field name confirmed correct; stub's prose description is unambiguous

Confidence: N/A (no issue)

`skf-forge-tier-rw.py` line 196 places `"qmd"` as a key under `tools` in the YAML output. The stub's "tools.qmd is true in `forge-tier.yaml`" matches the on-disk structure. Step-05 §5 uses the same gate ("forge tier is Deep AND QMD tool is available"). Consistent across all sites.

---

## All Other Areas — No Issues Found

**Behavior preservation (area 1):** All three reference files are strictly supersets of the inline text they replaced. The source-authority detection ref preserves all four fallback paths, adds the warn log for `gh api user` failure, and adds the case-insensitivity rationale. The portfolio-similarity ref preserves the enumeration approach, parallel query pattern, 3-highest-aggregate cut, heads-up format, and silent-skip-on-failure semantics. The draft-checkpoint ref preserves all fields in both the restore (Half 1) and write (Half 2), and the two field lists are symmetric.

**Resume flow-control (area 2):** The §7b skip rule is explicit in both the §6 stub (lines 219-220: "all of §7b are skipped") and in the ref (Half 1, lines 23-27: "skip the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b"). An LLM reading only the stub has sufficient information to skip §7b without loading the ref. The ref's Half 1 repeats the rule for self-contained correctness. No regression.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Draft Checkpoint Lifecycle

The `.brief-draft.json` file at `{forge_data_folder}/{skill-name}/.brief-draft.json` is a step-01 in-flight-state checkpoint. It exists only while the workflow has progressed past §7 but not yet completed step-05 — once the final brief writes successfully, step-05 §4 removes it.

**Headless mode skips this entire lifecycle** — the run completes in a single invocation, so no resume is meaningful and no checkpoint is written.

The two halves of the lifecycle (resume on entry, write on §7 confirmation) form a pair. This file documents both so a single load covers them.

## Half 1 — Resume Check (loaded from §6 after name confirmation)

After the skill name is confirmed in §6, check for an in-progress draft at `{forge_data_folder}/{name}/.brief-draft.json`. **Only present the resume prompt** when the file exists AND no `skill-brief.yaml` sits beside it (a finished brief uses the same dir; if a finished brief exists, the draft is stale and step-05's overwrite gate is the right control point).

When the precondition is met, present:

```
**An in-progress draft for `{name}` was found** (last updated: {mtime}).
  [Y] Resume from the saved draft (jump to §8 with prior answers restored)
  [N] Start fresh (delete the draft and re-gather)
```

### `[Y]` — Resume

Load the JSON and restore the captured fields: `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`, `target_version`, `doc_urls`, `intent`, `scope_hint`, `description`, `forge_tier`, `tier_source`. Then jump directly to §8 — **skip the rest of §6, all of §7, and all of §7b**.

The skip rule for §7b is load-bearing: re-running §7b would overwrite the user's previously accepted `description` with a fresh candidate synthesized from the seed material. The restored `description` is authoritative.

The user can still revise any field at step-04 §3 if a refinement is needed after the full brief is visible.

### `[N]` — Start fresh

Delete `.brief-draft.json` and continue with §6 normally (collision check, portfolio-similarity check if applicable, then §7 / §7b proceed in their normal order).

## Half 2 — Checkpoint Write (loaded from §7 after summary confirmation)

After the user confirms the §7 summary, persist the captured state atomically. Write a single JSON object with all of:

- `target_repo`, `source_type`, `source_authority`
- `target_version` (if set)
- `doc_urls` (if collected)
- `intent`, `scope_hint`
- `description` (the §7b accepted text)
- `forge_tier`, `tier_source` (for diagnostics)

Atomic-write protocol: write to `.brief-draft.json.tmp` first, then `mv .brief-draft.json.tmp .brief-draft.json`. The rename is atomic on a single filesystem; a partial write never becomes visible as `.brief-draft.json`.

The file is removed by step-05 §4 after the final brief writes successfully.
